sentence: Subjective:
HPI: Ashley James, a 14-year-old female, presents with a history of abdominal pain and vomiting, initially thought to be appendicitis, but later found to have an atrophic right kidney on ultrasound.
She has a history of recurrent infections as a child, with hospitalizations at 4 years old for 3-4 times, with symptoms of fever and vomiting, but no specific diagnosis made.
She has been taking Pepcid 40mg daily for symptoms and Claritin for allergies.
Her family history includes renal disease, with her father having a kidney transplant, aunt with end-stage renal disease, and mother with breast cancer.
Ashley's father died at 47 due to a massive heart attack while on dialysis.
Past medical history: Ashley has a history of recurrent infections as a child, with hospitalizations at 4 years old for 3-4 times, with symptoms of fever and vomiting.
Her father had a kidney transplant and died at 47 due to a massive heart attack while on dialysis.
Her aunt has end-stage renal disease, chronic kidney disease, stage V, diabetes, and hypertension.
Ashley's mother has breast cancer.
Review of systems: Ashley complains of abdominal pain, vomiting, and burning sensation when urinating, especially after consuming certain drinks like soda.
Current medications: Pepcid 40mg daily, Claritin for allergies.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: Ashley is a well-appearing female, alert and oriented, with no acute distress.
Her physical exam is within normal limits.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: Ashley has a history of recurrent infections and an atrophic right kidney, with a family history of renal disease.
Problem list: Atrophic right kidney; Recurrent infections; End-stage renal disease; Chronic kidney disease; Stage V; Diabetes; Hypertension.
Plan:
I recommended that Ashley undergo an annual ultrasound to monitor the size of her kidneys and their overall function.
I also suggested completing a urinalysis to ensure that protein and blood are not present in her urine, and a blood pressure check.
I advised avoiding nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory medicines like ibuprofen and opting for Tylenol instead.
I also recommended that Ashley stay well-hydrated, drinking at least 2L of water per day, and maintain a healthy diet to support her kidney health.
Follow-up:
Ashley can schedule an appointment with her primary care physician or an adult nephrologist closer to her home, rather than traveling to Atlanta.
